skill.name: github-release-management
skill.description: Guide GitHub release workflows with GitHub CLI, Claude Flow, CI validation, changelog generation, artifact publishing, and rollback planning.
skill.summary: Plan, validate, publish, monitor, and roll back GitHub releases with structured automation guidance.
skill.author: Claude Flow Team

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command execution, network, filesystem, and credential patterns. Review found these are release-management documentation examples for GitHub CLI, npm, Docker, Claude Flow, and GitHub Actions, not hidden executable code, but they can perform high-impact publishing and secret-handling actions if followed. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: High-Impact Release Commands in Skill Instructions
medium_findings.description: The skill documents many Bash and GitHub CLI commands that can create releases, publish packages, push Docker images, deploy targets, and perform rollbacks. This is expected for release management, but users should review commands before execution because they can change repositories and production artifacts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command patterns are real and semantically tied to publishing and deployment workflows. They are legitimate for this skill, but still create operational risk if executed without review.

medium_findings.title: CI Examples Handle GitHub and npm Tokens
medium_findings.description: The GitHub Actions example authenticates GitHub CLI with a workflow token and writes an npm token into .npmrc before publishing. These are normal CI release steps, but they involve secrets and should use least-privilege tokens and ephemeral runners.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly use GitHub and npm secrets in a release workflow. The context is legitimate CI automation, so the risk is operational rather than evidence of theft.

medium_findings.title: Temporary Files Used for Release Context
medium_findings.description: The workflow stores release pull request and commit data under /tmp before generating changelogs. This is a common CI pattern, but temporary files may expose release metadata on shared or misconfigured runners.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The /tmp writes are directly shown and are plausible in CI. The data appears to be release metadata rather than credentials, so severity is moderate.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation and Registry URLs
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged fixed URLs for npm registry, GitHub documentation, project issues, discussions, and documentation. These URLs are expected references for a GitHub release skill and do not indicate data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to standard documentation, support, and registry destinations used by the described workflow. No suspicious external collection endpoint was found.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Misread as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal findings are relative Markdown links to related documentation. They do not execute file reads or writes and are safe as documentation references.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged ../ sequences appear inside Markdown links under Related Resources. No code path uses them to access filesystem content.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and C2 Keyword Findings Lack Malicious Context
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and C2 keyword patterns are triggered by release metadata and ordinary release-monitoring terminology. No evidence found of encryption misuse, command-and-control behavior, or hidden networking logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited areas are documentation text and workflow examples. I found no semantic evidence that these patterns implement cryptography or malicious control channels.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Release Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples use command substitution to derive tags, changelog text, and release bodies. This is standard shell usage, but unsafe inputs in release notes or tags could cause workflow errors if copied without validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Command substitution is present in executable examples. The surrounding context is legitimate release automation, so the concern is injection hardening and review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Registry Publishing and Docker Push Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow includes npm publish, Docker build, Docker push, and GitHub release upload commands. These operations can publish artifacts externally and should run only in controlled release pipelines.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are direct publishing actions. They match the skill purpose, but their side effects are significant enough to document as dangerous patterns.

dangerous_patterns.title: Heuristic Dangerous Combination Is Contextual
dangerous_patterns.description: The combined external command, network, credential, and filesystem indicators are real, but they appear in GitHub Actions and release workflow documentation. No evidence found of concealed code, unauthorized network exfiltration, or prompt injection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The combined capabilities would be dangerous in executable code. In this file they are documented examples, so publication is acceptable with user warnings.

content.value_statement: Release work often spans changelogs, version bumps, tests, artifacts, and rollback plans. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured prompts and workflows for GitHub release management.
actual_capabilities: Draft GitHub releases with generated notes and release titles.
Plan semantic version changes from commits, PRs, and breaking changes.
Coordinate validation steps such as linting, tests, builds, and security checks.
Guide GitHub Actions workflows for releases, hotfixes, artifact upload, and package publishing.
Outline rollback, monitoring, changelog, and announcement workflows.
limitations: Requires installed tools such as gh, git, Node.js, npm or yarn, and Claude Flow for full workflows.
Examples can modify repositories, publish packages, and deploy artifacts if executed directly.
Does not replace human approval for production releases or incident response decisions.
Repository-specific workflow files, permissions, and secrets must be reviewed before use.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Standard Release
use_cases.description: Generate a release branch plan, changelog outline, validation checklist, and GitHub release draft for a small repository.

use_cases.title: Coordinate CI Release Pipelines
use_cases.description: Design GitHub Actions steps for testing, artifact upload, registry publishing, and post-release validation.

use_cases.title: Plan Emergency Hotfixes
use_cases.description: Create a focused hotfix workflow with critical tests, version selection, release notes, and rollback monitoring.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Release Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to prepare a release plan for version [version]. Include version checks, changelog steps, required tests, and approval points.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Release Notes
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to turn merged PRs and commits since [previous tag] into release notes for [version]. Group features, fixes, breaking changes, and migration notes.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Release Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review our GitHub Actions release workflow. Check permissions, secret handling, artifact publishing, validation steps, and rollback readiness.

prompt_templates.title: Orchestrate a Multi-Target Release
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan a release for GitHub, npm, Docker, and documentation. Include sequencing, gates, monitoring, failure handling, and rollback commands to review.

output_examples.input: Prepare release v2.4.0 from main with generated notes and validation.
output: A release branch plan with version updates and changelog generation.
A validation checklist covering install, lint, tests, build, and security scanning.
A draft GitHub release sequence with approval before publishing.

output_examples.input: Review our GitHub Actions release job before production use.
output: A permissions review for repository contents, packages, pull requests, and issues.
A secret-handling review for GitHub and npm tokens.
A list of release gates and rollback checks to add before deployment.

output_examples.input: Create an emergency hotfix process for a critical issue.
output: A hotfix branch and versioning plan based on the last stable release.
A reduced validation path for critical tests and builds.
A communication and monitoring checklist for post-release follow-up.

best_practices: Review every generated command before execution, especially publish, deploy, and rollback commands.
Use least-privilege GitHub and registry tokens with short lifetimes where possible.
Keep release gates explicit: tests, security scans, artifact verification, approvals, and monitoring.
anti_patterns: Do not run release commands from untrusted branches without reviewing workflow permissions.
Do not store long-lived registry tokens in repository files or logs.
Do not automate production releases without rollback criteria and human approval gates.

faq.question: Does this skill publish releases by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides instructions and examples. Actual publishing happens only when a user or automation runs the generated commands.

faq.question: Which tools are required?
faq.answer: The examples assume GitHub CLI, git, Node.js, npm or yarn, and optional Claude Flow or swarm tooling.

faq.question: Can it help with semantic versioning?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides version analysis using commits, PR labels, compatibility checks, and breaking change review.

faq.question: Can it create GitHub Actions workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes workflow patterns for release builds, changelog generation, artifact upload, registry publishing, and monitoring.

faq.question: Is it safe for production repositories?
faq.answer: Use it with review. The workflows can publish artifacts and use secrets, so permissions and commands must be checked before execution.

faq.question: Does it support rollback planning?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes rollback strategy, monitoring, health checks, and hotfix workflow guidance.
